findings.description: Page layout is not preserved; the formal letterhead layout is converted into a simplified single-column flow.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 1, governor/secretary/director names appear in a left-side block with a centered seal/header and positioned date/address blocks. In the HTML, these elements are flattened into a linear top-to-bottom arrangement, losing the original spatial structure.

findings.description: Text formatting is only partially preserved on the first letter page.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows distinct indentation for the quoted paragraph and stronger separation of header/address blocks; the HTML keeps bold/underline in places but reduces indentation and visual hierarchy.

findings.description: Second page of the letter loses original placement and spacing of signature block and closing content.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 2, 'Certified Mail / Mr. Threat / Page Two' appears compact at top left with body text and signature positioned below. In the HTML, content is spread differently with the signature/closing appearing in altered spacing and page composition.

findings.description: Signature graphic placement/appearance differs from the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF contains a clearly placed handwritten signature over the sign-off area. In the HTML, the signature area appears repositioned and visually reduced within the reflowed page.

findings.description: Email page layout is significantly altered, with embedded screenshot and surrounding text not preserving original composition.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 3, the email header fields, caution banner, body, signature block, and prior message are arranged as a conventional email printout. In the HTML, the same content is reflowed with different spacing and an inserted reduced image snapshot embedded mid-page, changing the original structure.

findings.description: Graphics are duplicated/reduced and repositioned within the email conversion.
findings.evidence: The PDF page shows one coherent email rendering. The HTML includes a smaller embedded image of the email content in addition to live text, creating a duplicated visual artifact not present in the source.

findings.description: Reading order is disrupted by duplicate embedded image content preceding repeated text.
findings.evidence: A sighted reader of the HTML encounters the main email body and also a miniature inserted version of the same content before continuing, unlike the PDF's single continuous reading flow.

findings.description: Email thread layout is not preserved; messages are reflowed and duplicated visually.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 4, the message from Brian Wrenn and quoted prior message form a clear sequential email printout. In the HTML, the content is reorganized with repeated header/body material and altered spacing.

findings.description: Reading order becomes confusing due to repeated email segments.
findings.evidence: The HTML presents the Wrenn message and quoted Piazza message in a way that appears duplicated compared with the PDF's single sequence.

findings.description: Structured numbered updates are visually duplicated and reflowed.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 5, the numbered list 1-5 is presented once in a clean memo/email layout. In the HTML, the page includes both a rendered page image and repeated live text below, altering the original page structure.

findings.description: Formatting of numbered list items and emphasized text is weakened by reflow.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses clearer indentation and bold/italic emphasis within numbered items. The HTML retains some emphasis but the list is visually flatter and less distinct.

findings.description: Reading order is impaired by duplicated list content.
findings.evidence: Readers encounter the numbered section once as an image-like rendering and again as text in the HTML, unlike the PDF's single presentation.

findings.description: Page layout for the continuation email is altered, with duplicated content blocks and changed spacing.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 6, the continuation of the Rep. Dennis Riddell email appears as a single coherent page. In the HTML, portions of the page appear both as inserted reduced renderings and as reflowed text.

findings.description: Email signature and related graphic elements are visually reduced/repositioned.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows the signature block and seal in a consistent email print format. In the HTML, these visual elements are smaller and displaced within the reflow.

findings.description: Reading flow is disrupted by duplicated continuation content.
findings.evidence: The HTML presents content from the page in more than one visual form, unlike the PDF's straightforward sequence.

findings.description: Letterhead layout on Colonial Pipeline letter is not preserved.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 7, the Colonial Pipeline logo, company name, sender block, and contact information are positioned in a formal letterhead arrangement. In the HTML, these pieces are separated and partially duplicated, losing the original structure.

findings.description: Logo and letterhead graphics are repositioned and partially duplicated.
findings.evidence: The PDF displays one clean logo/header. The HTML shows the logo and company header in altered positions with extra rendered fragments of the source page.

findings.description: Continuation page of the Colonial letter is reflowed and mixed with a reduced page image.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 8, the body, signature, attachment note, and cc list appear in a single structured closing page. In the HTML, live text appears above a reduced image-like rendering of the signed page, changing the layout substantially.

findings.description: Signature block appearance differs from the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF contains a visible handwritten signature in the closing. In the HTML, the signature appears mainly within a reduced embedded rendering rather than integrated cleanly in the text flow.

findings.description: Reading order is affected by duplicate presentation of the same closing content.
findings.evidence: A reader sees the closing letter text and then a smaller rendering of the same page below, unlike the PDF where the content appears once.

findings.description: Attachment title page and first attachment page are combined/reflowed rather than preserved as distinct pages.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 9, the attachment page begins cleanly with 'ATTACHMENT 1' and page 1 of the terms. In the HTML, the preceding letter text remains above, and the attachment title/page content appears lower as part of a continuous scroll.

findings.description: Hierarchy of attachment heading and legal formatting is softened.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses centered headings, underlining, and compact legal-document formatting. The HTML keeps content but with flatter hierarchy and more generic body styling.

findings.description: Second attachment page is not preserved as a standalone page and is visually mixed with prior content.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 10, page 2 of Attachment 1 is presented clearly with sections C-J. In the HTML, page 1 remnants and page 2 content appear within one long reflowed page, reducing separation.

findings.description: Legal section formatting and pagination cues are weakened.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses compact section lettering and page numbering. In the HTML, the text remains readable but loses the same legal-document visual discipline.

findings.description: Third attachment page is reflowed and visually merged with content from prior page.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 11, section K-M and page number 3 appear on a distinct page. In the HTML, the continuation from page 2 and page 3 run together in one scrolling layout.

findings.description: Reading order is slightly harder to follow because page boundaries are not respected for the legal attachment.
findings.evidence: The PDF's clear transition from J to K-M across pages is flattened in HTML into a continuous block, making original document sequencing less explicit.

findings.description: Fourth attachment page is reflowed and combined with previous material instead of preserving standalone structure.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 12, items 3-4 and Section II are presented clearly on page 4. In the HTML, this page is appended into the continuing text flow with altered spacing and page separation.

findings.description: Exhibits title page is not materially wrong but page separation is reduced in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF page 13 shows a mostly blank page with centered exhibits heading and page number. HTML presents the same content in continuous flow, with less page-like isolation.

findings.description: Exhibit 1A page layout is not preserved; the page image is shifted and followed by duplicated caption text.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 14, the exhibit drawing occupies the page with caption below. In the HTML, the exhibit appears lower on the page with repeated captioning and altered spacing.

findings.description: Exhibit image is present but placement and surrounding captioning differ from the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF contains one large exhibit figure. The HTML includes the figure but separated by extra whitespace and repeated textual caption elements.

findings.description: Exhibit 1B page is presented with duplicated page number/caption flow and altered image placement.
findings.evidence: On PDF page 15, the close-up exhibit appears centered with caption beneath. In the HTML, page numbering/caption text appears repeated around the image and the exhibit is embedded lower in the flow.

findings.description: Exhibit image remains present and legible but is not positioned like the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows a single exhibit image and caption. The HTML shows the image with altered margins and repeated caption/page artifacts.

findings.description: The HTML adds fabricated CSS/theme metadata and a document title not present in the PDF.
findings.evidence: HTML includes: "/* Digital Commons NC — Standalone Theme CSS */", CSS variables such as "--nc-font", "--nc-navy", and <title>Converted Document</title>. These elements are not part of the source PDF content.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy in the HTML does not reflect the document structure and uses multiple h1/h2/h3 headings for content that is not consistently structured as headings in the PDF.
findings.evidence:
HTML uses

"Colonial Pipeline Company"

,

"Wehner, Judy"

,

"ATTACHMENT 1"

, and multiple

headings for letter lines and section captions. In the PDF, these are largely letterhead, page labels, or formatted section titles rather than a normalized HTML heading hierarchy.

findings.description: Some alt text is generic or empty rather than descriptive.
findings.evidence: HTML contains several images with alt="" and others such as alt="Social Media Icons" or alt="" for page images/maps, while the PDF includes meaningful visual content like maps, seals, and signatures.

findings.description: There are text accuracy errors and OCR/conversion mistakes that alter wording or reduce fidelity.
findings.evidence: Examples from HTML: "Alamance Aggregate Quarry" instead of PDF context referring to "Alamance Aggregates"; "Please contact me at (919) 707-9220" whereas the PDF shows 707-9220 but formatting/context may differ across pages; HTML says "please feel fee to contact Judy or me" which appears to be an OCR typo for "please feel free"; HTML footer line renders the address as "1185 Sanctuary Parkway, 2nd Floor Alpharetta, Georgia 30009" without the comma visible in the PDF layout. These are conversion inaccuracies beyond simple reflow.
